title: One More Final You can not Shut Your Eyes
display_title: One More Final You can not Shut Your Eyes
wikitext:
[]: One More Final: You can (not) Shut Your Eyes             Quote Post by ran1 » Wed Jul 07, 2010 7:03 pm Today on the IRC, myself, Chee, Essel, and Anti-Goth were discussing the nature of One More Final, specifically the actions of Asuka and Shinji in it. I had made a comment in passing about the similarities of One More Final and Jean Paul Sartre's great existentialist play, No Exit. After some more consideration on the matter, I am now under the impression that One More Final is indeed inspired by the play, and I wouldn't put it out of Anno's directorial prowess to have made his ending scene of his film an encapsulation of the play's themes Part 1: No Exit – A (Very) Short Introduction Here is a link to the play. It's not an especially long play, and it's only in one act. It is an excellent play regardless of its possible relationship to the film, and I recommend it for anyone who liked EoE. Anyone familiar with the play can skip this part. As far as summaries are concerned, here goes: No Exit is a one act play that takes place in Hell. But not Hell in the more common mythological/theological sense – Sartre's play posits Hell as a bourgeoisie drawing room with three sofas and three occupants. They are Garcin, male, Estelle, female, and Inez, female. When the three make their arrivals, they are informed by Hell's valet that they must spend the remainder of eternity together. None of the occupants know each other from previous lives, so they make a point of introducing themselves with great care, at first not revealing their “bad” qualities that earned them their seat in Hell to begin with. Garcin comes off as the reserved, submissive male, Estelle as the arrogant ditzy girl, and Inez as quiet and distant, who seems to be grappling with her own set of inner demons. As the trio's stay in hell continues, they slowly bring themselves, both consciously and unconsciously, to lay out their emotional cards on the table and reveal their “true selves”. A one sided rivalry between Estelle and Inez developed (fueled by Estelle) over Garcin's affections. Estelle makes advances on the emotionally broken Garcin, who cannot get over the cowardice he displayed in his previous life. Their paths spiral downward, and by the play's climax, all three come to the conclusion that they hate each other. Garcin lays emotionally wasted and Estelle and Inez are literally at each other's throats. During this climax, Garcin realizes that the three of them will inevitably go on hating each other forever, and according to him, “Hell... is not fire and brimstone.... Hell is other people!” Short Thematic Analysis The final conclusion reached by Garcin is that “hell is other people” – or better put “hell is the incomplete and failed attempt at human-to-human communication”. The play, not surprisingly, is rife with lies, incomplete truths, and failure of communication, and of course, this leads to the to the misunderstandings that eventually drive the characters to the point of outright violence and hatred. The final message of the play is seemingly nihilistic to most audiences, but when looked at from Sartre's worldview, where, according to him, “all human action by principle is doomed to failure” the play comes off more as an acknowledgment and resignation of the inability to communicate feelings rather than a fearful and miserable contemplation. Part 2: Similarities to One More Final 1. You Can (not) Shut Your Eyes [wkimg width=400]File:M26 C469 Wideeyed Shinji.jpg[/wkimg] [wkimg width=400]File:M26 C478 d Choking my Asuka.jpg[/wkimg] No Exit wrote: VALET: Romantic, that's what you are. GARCIN: Will you keep quiet, please! . . . I won't make a scene, I shan't be sorry for myself, I'll face the situation, as I said just now. Face it fairly and squarely. I won't have it springing at me from behind, before I've time to size it up. And you call that being "romantic!" . . . So it comes to this; one doesn't need rest. Why bother about sleep if one isn't sleepy? That stands to reason, doesn't it? Wait a minute, there's a snag somewhere; something disagreeable. Why, now, should it be disagreeable? . . . Ah, I see; it's life without a break. VALET: What are you talking about? GARCIN: Your eyelids. We move ours up and down. Blinking, we call it. It's like a small black shutter that clicks down and makes a break. Everything goes black; one's eyes are moistened. You can't imagine how restful, refreshing, it is. Four thousand little rests per hour. Four thousand little respites--just think! . . . So that's the idea. I'm to live without eyelids. Don't act the fool, you know what I mean. No eyelids, no sleep; it follows, doesn't it? I shall never sleep again. But then--how shall I endure my own company?

[]: Re: One More Final: You can (not) Shut Your Eyes             Quote Post by EvangelionFan » Wed Jul 07, 2010 7:13 pm This is the first time I've heard of this play and from your analysis I think the similarities are valid. As to whether Anno took inspiration from the play I guess is something I'm not sure about (this is mostly because I'm not familiar with Anno's mindset and/or approach to writing and directing outside of TV+EoE). ran1 wrote: Essentially: Garcin ~ Shinji Estelle ~ Asuka Inez ~ Rei

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Wed Jul 07, 2010 7:31 pm Oh, it's not a completely new comparison... The Play by Chenalos

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Wed Jul 07, 2010 8:46 pm As usual, this is quite an interesting perspective on NGE, Ran. I think, perhaps, the similarities might be a little broad, but that doesn't necessarily make them invalid. Anno is, afterall, tackling a lot of broad existentialist-heavy themes and it makes sense that one of the seminal existentialist philosophers would have resonant in the work. I said in a thread on IMDB that I don't think anyone really knows what Anno's direct influences were. I suspect most were unconscious and that Anno was just synthesizing a lifetime of diffused influences from various aspects of the world of art. This usually means that the similarities between it and other works are going to be plentiful. I see more than a few similarities between NGE and Hamlet, for instance.

[]:             Quote Post by child of Lilith » Wed Jul 07, 2010 8:47 pm I’ve never seen the play, but if what you say is true then it really does seem to fit with the ending to EOE. Though I think the ending to EOE is a little more hopeful then the ending to the play.

[]: Because of you. Because of you. Because of you.             Quote Post by Killer Bee » Wed Jul 07, 2010 11:16 pm I've been meaning to read No Exit for a long time now, so I'm glad you brought this up. I just read it, and saw the similarities between Garcin and Shinji, and Estelle and Asuka at once. Inez, however, is a little hard to place in the puzzle. I didn't really see much of a connection with Rei, aside from the "silent observer" aspect which a lot of characters in Evangelion tend to embody at one point or another. The dialogue in No Exit is very similar to the scrip of EoTV in the way that it's worded and presented. INEZ: Did he shoot himself on your account? ESTELLE: Of course not. How absurd you are! GARCIN: Then why should you have been so scared? He blew his brains out, didn't he? That's how his face got smashed. ESTELLE: Don't! Please don't go on. GARCIN: Because of you. Because of you. INEZ: He shot himself because of you. ESTELLE: Leave me alone! It's--it's not fair, bullying me like that. I want to go! I want to go!

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Thu Jul 08, 2010 12:39 am See also: Tines' POV on the ending that he's had for all these years.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Thu Jul 08, 2010 1:23 am Sailor Star Dust wrote: See also: Tines' POV on the ending that he's had for all these years.

[]:             Quote Post by carla » Thu Jul 08, 2010 10:55 am i'd read no exit before and i definitely agree, "hell is other people" is absolutely the pervasive theme in both no exit and 1MF. thanks for this, ran, it's very nice to see it all laid out like this. much like other people have commented, i have to say that i don't see much of a parallel between Rei and inez. they're both observers, but 1MF!Rei is a lot less of an active participant than inez is. i don't know if i would even parallel Rei to the valet-- bookend!Rei is just sort of... there. in my mind by that point Rei had already transcended lilim existence altogether, so i wouldn't really call her a participant in it, not even a neutral participant. also, i like the point about the eyelids... although i would guess anno didn't do that intentionally (i figured it was more the shock of everything that was happening-- wide-eyed was pretty much Shinji's natural state from the second he saw Asuka's mangled eva down to the very last second of EoE, tangification notwithstanding, of course) . but it's a nice comparison, certainly sends chills down my spine now that i think about it. how do we know Asuka was unconscious, btw? i always figured she was just quiet. maybe i just missed something.

[]:             Quote Post by wanderingknight » Thu Jul 08, 2010 1:56 pm Considering NGE's concept of an individual draws heavily from Sartre's conception of the Being ( consciousness , using Sartre's terminology) this is hardly surprising. Though the relationship between what Anno portrays in NGE and Sartre's philosophy (prior to Critique of dialectical reason , focusing more on Being and nothingness , his first philosophical work) is far, far deeper than just the phrase "Hell is other people".

[]:             Quote Post by planet news » Sun Jul 11, 2010 12:11 am This is just awesome goddammit. I have nothing to add. Just sayin' that this is an awesome connection.

[]:             Quote Post by berto » Sun Jul 11, 2010 4:04 am I do remember this. I don't know if agree with it %100 but I can deffinitly see the resemblence. There is one thing I saw at the end of the movie, I don't think I ever acctually talked about it but when I saw the end it reminded me of the last chapters of the Time Machine . Near the end of the book the travaler looses conciousness while ussing the machine. When he wakes up he is so far into the future the world is no longer the same. He stops at a deserted beach to find humans are no longer humans, Mercury is now so close it can be seen with the naked eye, and the sun itself has stoped and begon to dim. The travaler returns home, gets off the time machine, sits down on a corner, and beggins shaking uncotrolably. It was the shock of what he just saw. I always thought that that was the reason they looked onward like that, the shock of what they just witnessed. Anyway, back to Sartre. Near the end of the story the show becomes the pinicle of the Sartre's theme in the play, intentional or otherwise, it's not just the case with three people but the entire cast. However I do think that the most important diference in the stories is how Asuka touched Shinji's face. There is a sence of progression in that, a sence of forgiveness. It is the glimer of hope that maybe they'll be ok. While the play only portraits a messege of eternal emotional pain the movie shows the hope that someday people will understand each other.

[]:             Quote Post by chaosakita » Sun Jul 11, 2010 10:35 pm I think one can always find similarities between characters between stories, but unless it's a blatant ripoffs, those are just similarities and perhaps archetypes. I guess it's interesting that both of the two main male characters are both cowards and desire to have themselves to be validated as otherwise, but I think it's just a common theme throughout many works. I don't know about how much Western literature is taught throughout Japan, so I have no idea if Anno was inspired by the work, but I think that they have fundamentally different meanings. Like berto below me, I think Asuka's touch is a affirmation of the power of connections between two people, rather than the opposite.

[]:             Quote Post by Sun Stealer » Sun Jul 18, 2010 9:02 pm The final conclusion reached by Garcin is that “hell is other people” – or better put “hell is the incomplete and failed attempt at human-to-human communication”. The play, not surprisingly, is rife with lies, incomplete truths, and failure of communication, and of course, this leads to the to the misunderstandings that eventually drive the characters to the point of outright violence and hatred. The final message of the play is seemingly nihilistic to most audiences, but when looked at from Sartre's worldview, where, according to him, “all human action by principle is doomed to failure” the play comes off more as an acknowledgment and resignation of the inability to communicate feelings rather than a fearful and miserable contemplation.

[]:             Quote Post by berto » Mon Jul 19, 2010 12:50 pm You know it's the greater tragedies that in both stories if only the characters were brave enough to amit to their faults and sins they might find salvation, but since they can't they are stuck in this infinite cycle of self destruction and incomplete relationships, unable to move forward.

[]:             Quote Post by Ornette » Sun Jul 25, 2010 8:54 pm Locked by the request of the topic starter.

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Sat Aug 21, 2010 3:46 pm Unlocked at the topic starter's request.

[]:             Quote Post by Angel of Will » Tue Apr 03, 2012 10:28 pm Right at the start of EoE's Instrumentality, there is a green exit sign (which was also seen in Episode 25 in the theater), that is slapped away with the smack of a hand almost as soon as it appears. I believe this is a reference to No Exit. At the very least, the symbolism is meant to convey that there is no more escape for Shinji. I found that Episodes 25 and 26 were very reminiscent of the play.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Wed Apr 04, 2012 4:05 pm Sun Stealer wrote: Yes, but EoE does not end with the strangling. It ends with Asuka caressing Shinji's cheek, Shinji stops strangling her and breaks down into tears, Asuka gives her famous line. The message in the final scene of EoE doesn't seem to be "Hell is other people" but, "people may be intrinsically flawed and incapable of perfect communication, but they're good enough."
